title: Supreme Court Recognises Right to Walk on Footpaths Amid Surge in Pedestrian Deaths
summary: The Supreme Court of India issued an order recognising the right to walk on footpaths as a fundamental right, linked to the right to life under Article 21 of the Constitution. The order comes against the backdrop of a significant surge in pedestrian deaths in India, with footpaths frequently encroached upon or absent. The court's intervention highlights the state's obligation to ensure safe pedestrian infrastructure in urban and rural areas.
exam_angle: Connects to the expansive judicial interpretation of Article 21 (Right to Life) and the Supreme Court's role in enforcing fundamental rights related to urban governance and public safety.
key_facts: Right to walk on footpaths linked to Article 21 (Right to Life)
Supreme Court order addresses surge in pedestrian fatalities in India
Encroachment of footpaths identified as a core civic governance failure
Reflects expansive judicial interpretation of fundamental rights

title: Supreme Court Examines Whether Police Need Court Permission for Further Investigation After Filing Final Report
summary: The Supreme Court examined the legal question of whether police require court permission to conduct further investigation after filing a final report (charge sheet) under the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC). This is a significant procedural question under Section 173(8) CrPC, which allows further investigation even after filing a final report. The ruling has implications for the rights of accused persons and investigative powers of the police.
exam_angle: Directly relevant to criminal procedure law under CrPC Section 173(8); tests understanding of police powers post-final report and judicial oversight of investigation.
key_facts: Issue involves Section 173(8) of CrPC (now BNSS equivalent)
Question: Can police conduct further investigation without court permission after final report?
Involves balance between investigative powers and rights of the accused
Supreme Court is the adjudicating body

title: Delhi High Court Orders Centre to Remove Fake Posts About CJI and Ministers at UK Event
summary: The Delhi High Court directed the Central Government to take down social media posts falsely claiming that the Chief Justice of India (CJI) and Union Ministers were playing badminton at an event in the United Kingdom. The court found the posts to be fabricated and potentially damaging to constitutional authorities. The order touches upon issues of intermediary liability, disinformation, and protection of the dignity of constitutional offices.
exam_angle: Relevant to free speech vs. constitutional dignity, IT Act provisions on fake/defamatory content, and the judiciary's power to order content takedowns; also relates to contempt of court.
key_facts: Delhi High Court issued takedown order to the Centre
Posts falsely depicted CJI and ministers at a UK badminton event
Raises issues under IT Act regarding fake/defamatory social media content
Concerns protection of dignity of constitutional offices

title: Supreme Court Dismisses Plea Challenging ED Searches in TASMAC Liquor Case
summary: The Supreme Court rejected a businessman's petition challenging the legality of Enforcement Directorate (ED) searches conducted in connection with the Tamil Nadu State Marketing Corporation (TASMAC) liquor scam case. The court upheld the ED's authority to conduct searches under the Prevention of Money Laundering Act (PMLA). The TASMAC case involves alleged irregularities in the procurement and distribution of liquor in Tamil Nadu.
exam_angle: Tests knowledge of ED's powers under PMLA, including search and seizure provisions; also relevant to federalism as TASMAC is a state government entity.
key_facts: ED conducted searches under Prevention of Money Laundering Act (PMLA)
TASMAC: Tamil Nadu State Marketing Corporation (state liquor body)
Supreme Court upheld ED's search authority
Petition by a businessman challenging the searches was dismissed

title: Parliamentary Panel to Study India's Evolving Economic Conditions Amid Global Uncertainties
summary: A Parliamentary Standing Committee has decided to examine India's evolving economic conditions in the context of global uncertainties, including geopolitical tensions, trade disruptions, and concerns about growth slowdown. The committee will study macroeconomic indicators, fiscal health, and the impact of external factors on India's growth trajectory. This comes at a time when India is navigating tariff disputes, currency pressures, and shifting global supply chains.
exam_angle: Relevant to the role of Parliamentary Standing Committees in economic oversight; also connects to themes of macroeconomic management, fiscal policy, and India's economic resilience.
key_facts: Parliamentary Standing Committee to examine India's macroeconomic outlook
Focus on impact of global uncertainties on India's growth
Issues include geopolitical tensions, trade disruptions, and fiscal health
Reflects Parliament's oversight role on economic policy

title: India Seeks Tariff Advantage Over Rivals Before Finalising Trade Pact with the US
summary: India is reportedly seeking a preferential tariff advantage over competitor nations such as China and other Asian economies before formally concluding a trade agreement with the United States. New Delhi wants assurances that Indian exports will face lower tariffs than rivals in key sectors before enacting the pact. The negotiations are part of broader India-US bilateral trade discussions aimed at resolving the tariff disputes initiated under the Trump administration.
exam_angle: Directly relevant to India-US trade relations, WTO most-favoured-nation (MFN) principles, and India's trade diplomacy strategy; important for GS Paper 2 (IR) and GS Paper 3 (Economy).
key_facts: India seeking tariff edge over China and Asian rivals in US trade deal
Negotiations part of India-US bilateral trade agreement talks
India wants lower tariff rates than competitors before signing pact
Context: Trump-era tariff disputes and reciprocal tariff framework

title: US-Iran Nuclear Talks Resume; Trump Warns of Tolls on Strait of Hormuz if Negotiations Fail
summary: The United States and Iran resumed direct diplomatic dialogue on Iran's nuclear programme, with President Donald Trump warning that the US could impose tolls on the Strait of Hormuz if talks collapse. The Strait of Hormuz is a critical global oil chokepoint through which approximately 20% of the world's oil supply passes. The talks are part of ongoing diplomatic efforts to revive a nuclear deal following the collapse of the JCPOA.
exam_angle: The Strait of Hormuz is a critical geography topic (oil chokepoint); Iran nuclear deal (JCPOA) is a recurring IR topic; energy security implications for India which imports significant oil from the Gulf.
key_facts: Strait of Hormuz: ~20% of global oil supply passes through it
Trump threatened 'tolls' on Hormuz if US-Iran talks fail
Talks aimed at reviving nuclear deal (JCPOA framework)
Critical for India's energy security given Gulf oil imports

title: International Yoga Day 2025: Global Events Mark June 21 Celebration
summary: Thousands of people across the world participated in events marking International Yoga Day on June 21, 2025. The United Nations declared June 21 as International Yoga Day in 2014 following a proposal by Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi, and it has been observed globally since 2015. This year marks the 11th International Yoga Day, with events held across continents celebrating India's ancient yogic tradition.
exam_angle: International Yoga Day is a standard 'culture and soft power' question; connects to India's cultural diplomacy, UNESCO intangible heritage, and the UN's role in recognising Indian traditions.
key_facts: International Yoga Day: June 21 every year
UN declared it in December 2014 on PM Modi's proposal
First observed globally on June 21, 2015
2025 marks the 11th International Yoga Day

title: Trump States Anthropic No Longer Viewed as US National Security Threat
summary: US President Donald Trump told media outlet Axios that he no longer considers Anthropic, the AI safety company behind the Claude large language model, to be a national security threat. This marks a shift from earlier concerns about AI companies with foreign investment ties. Anthropic had faced scrutiny due to investments from non-US entities; the development signals a potential easing of AI regulatory pressure in the United States.
exam_angle: Relevant to AI governance, national security implications of AI companies, and the geopolitics of artificial intelligence regulation — a growing area in GS Paper 3 (Sci-Tech) and GS Paper 2 (IR).
key_facts: Anthropic: US-based AI safety company, maker of Claude LLM
Trump: Anthropic no longer a national security threat
Earlier concerns related to foreign investment in US AI firms
Reflects evolving US policy on AI governance and national security
